skill.name: latchbio-integration
skill.description: Guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through building, configuring, and deploying bioinformatics workflows on the Latch platform.
skill.summary: Build and deploy Latch bioinformatics workflows with SDK, data, resource, and verified workflow guidance.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, network, filesystem, secret, and weak-crypto patterns. Review found these are documentation examples for Latch SDK workflows, not hidden executable skill code or prompt injection. The skill remains medium risk because it instructs users to install packages, authenticate to Latch, register cloud workflows, move files between local and cloud storage, and retrieve secrets.

medium_findings.title: Cloud Workflow Registration and External Commands
medium_findings.description: The skill includes Latch CLI commands to install the SDK, authenticate, initialize workflows, and register workflows to the Latch platform. This is expected for the skill purpose, but it can execute local tooling, build containers, and upload workflow artifacts to a cloud service, so user approval and workspace review are needed before running commands.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands and registration behavior are directly documented and match the skill purpose. They are not malicious, but they create real operational risk because they affect the local environment and a remote platform.

medium_findings.title: Cloud Data Transfer and Secret Access Guidance
medium_findings.description: The documentation shows LatchFile and LatchDir automatically moving files between local execution and cloud storage, plus a get_secret example for retrieving workflow secrets. These are legitimate Latch features, but they require careful handling of regulated research data and credentials.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file transfer and secret retrieval examples are explicit in the documentation. No exfiltration endpoint is present, but misuse could expose data or credentials through normal platform operations.

low_findings.title: Static Command Pattern False Positives in Markdown
low_findings.description: Most external command detections are caused by Markdown code fences, inline code spans, decorators, and CLI examples. No Ruby backtick execution or hidden script body was found in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed locations are documentation examples rather than executable Ruby or shell interpolation. The static signature appears to have matched Markdown syntax and command snippets.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Pattern False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak-cryptography detections align with bioinformatics terms, workflow names, and general documentation text rather than hashing or encryption implementation. No evidence found of cryptographic code or weak algorithm selection.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines discuss DESeq2, workflow design, or resource guidance. They do not implement MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or related cryptographic operations.

low_findings.title: No Prompt Injection Evidence Found
low_findings.description: Reviewed files did not contain override language, fake system instructions, pre-approval claims, or requests to skip security analysis. No prompt injection finding is warranted.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Targeted search for common prompt injection phrases returned no matches. The opening sections are normal skill and reference documentation.

dangerous_patterns.title: External CLI Operations
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill instructs users to run package installation, authentication, initialization, registration, and verbose troubleshooting commands. These operations should be run only after checking the target directory and account context.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are plainly documented and useful for the skill. They remain sensitive because they change local and remote state.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote Data and Secret Handling
dangerous_patterns.description: Examples cover automatic upload to latch paths and retrieval of named secrets. These patterns need user confirmation for protected datasets and credentials.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples are direct Latch platform usage, not hidden malicious code. The confidence is high for the behavior and moderate for security impact because safe use depends on user data and account policy.

content.value_statement: Bioinformatics teams need reproducible cloud workflows without rewriting platform details each time. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through Latch SDK workflow creation, data handling, resource sizing, and verified workflow use.
actual_capabilities: Explains how to create Latch workflows with workflow and task decorators.
Shows LatchFile and LatchDir patterns for local and cloud data paths.
Documents resource decorators for CPU, memory, GPU, timeout, and storage choices.
Guides workflow registration, execution, and troubleshooting with the Latch CLI.
Summarizes verified workflows for RNA-seq, DESeq2, AlphaFold, single-cell, and CRISPR analysis.
Describes Registry projects, tables, records, links, enums, transactions, and secrets.
limitations: It is documentation guidance, not an installed Latch client or authenticated connector.
It cannot verify current Latch platform limits or pricing without external documentation.
It does not validate biological correctness of generated workflows or parameters.
It may require Docker, Latch credentials, and user approval before commands can run.

use_cases.title: Create a Reproducible Analysis Pipeline
use_cases.description: Use the skill to draft a Latch workflow with typed inputs, task decorators, and registration steps for a genomics pipeline.

use_cases.title: Plan Cloud Data Organization
use_cases.description: Use the data management references to design Latch Registry tables, sample records, linked outputs, and latch path conventions.

use_cases.title: Right-Size Workflow Resources
use_cases.description: Use the resource guidance to choose CPU, memory, GPU, timeout, and temporary storage settings for each workflow stage.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Workflow
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a single-step workflow.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a simple Latch workflow for one input file and one output file. Use clear type annotations and explain the registration steps.

prompt_templates.title: Add Cloud Data Handling
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate workflow with LatchFile, LatchDir, and Registry usage.
prompt_templates.prompt: Update my workflow plan to use LatchFile inputs, a LatchDir output, and a Registry table for sample metadata. Include safe path conventions.

prompt_templates.title: Tune Resources for a Pipeline
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate resource planning for mixed workload stages.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this bioinformatics pipeline and recommend Latch task decorators for each stage. Consider CPU, memory, GPU, timeout, storage, and cost.

prompt_templates.title: Combine Verified and Custom Workflows
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced workflow design using verified workflows and custom steps.
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a Latch workflow that combines a verified RNA-seq or AlphaFold workflow with custom preprocessing and postprocessing. Identify data, resource, and validation risks.

output_examples.input: User asks for a Latch RNA-seq workflow outline.
output_examples.output: A staged plan with input FASTQ files, quality control, alignment, quantification, output paths, task sizes, and registration checks.

output_examples.input: User asks how to organize sample metadata in Latch.
output_examples.output: A Registry design with a project, sample table, result table, linked records, status values, and recommended naming conventions.

output_examples.input: User asks why a workflow ran out of memory.
output_examples.output: A troubleshooting checklist covering task decorator choice, memory allocation, temporary storage, timeout, logs, and local Docker testing.

best_practices: Confirm the Latch workspace, account, and target directory before running registration or upload commands.
Use small task resources first, then increase CPU, memory, GPU, timeout, or storage based on measured failures.
Keep sample metadata, output paths, and workflow versions consistent for reproducible analysis.
anti_patterns: Do not run latch register from an unreviewed directory or with unknown dependencies.
Do not store API keys or regulated data in source files, prompts, or plain workflow parameters.
Do not over-allocate GPU or memory resources before profiling the workflow workload.

faq.question: Does this skill run Latch commands by itself?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples. Any shell command still requires the user or agent environment to run it.

faq.question: Which AI tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.

faq.question: Can it help with Nextflow or Snakemake?
faq.answer: Yes. The documentation covers registering existing Nextflow and Snakemake pipelines with Latch.

faq.question: Can it manage secrets safely?
faq.answer: It documents Latch secret retrieval patterns, but users must configure and approve secrets through their trusted Latch workspace.

faq.question: Is this a verified Latch connector?
faq.answer: No. It is a community documentation skill, not an official authenticated connector.

faq.question: What security review result applies?
faq.answer: The skill is medium risk and publishable with warnings because it guides cloud workflow operations and credential-aware workflows.
